---
title: Probabilistic sensitivity analysis
---

Deterministic sensitivity analysis reads one parameter at a time; probabilistic sensitivity analysis (PSA) propagates the joint uncertainty in every parameter through the model at once. This tutorial puts distributions on the [Markov cohort model](markov-cohort.qmd), samples a parameter draw matrix, runs it through `run_psa`, and checks convergence. It assumes that tutorial. The model definition is repeated here so the page stands alone.

## The model

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from heormodel.models import CohortSpec, MarkovModel

STATES = ("H", "S1", "S2", "D")
STRATEGIES = ("Standard of care", "Strategy A", "Strategy B", "Strategy AB")

def r2p(rate):
    return 1.0 - np.exp(-np.asarray(rate))

def model(p, strategy):
    p_HS1, p_S1H, p_S1S2 = r2p(p["r_HS1"]), r2p(p["r_S1H"]), r2p(p["r_S1S2"])
    p_HD, p_S1D, p_S2D = r2p(p["r_HD"]), r2p(p["r_HD"] * p["hr_S1"]), r2p(p["r_HD"] * p["hr_S2"])
    prog = r2p(p["r_S1S2"] * p["hr_S1S2_trtB"]) if "B" in strategy else p_S1S2
    P = np.zeros((4, 4))
    P[0, 0], P[0, 1], P[0, 3] = (1 - p_HD) * (1 - p_HS1), (1 - p_HD) * p_HS1, p_HD
    P[1, 0], P[1, 2], P[1, 3] = (1 - p_S1D) * p_S1H, (1 - p_S1D) * prog, p_S1D
    P[1, 1] = (1 - p_S1D) * (1 - p_S1H - prog)
    P[2, 2], P[2, 3], P[3, 3] = 1 - p_S2D, p_S2D, 1.0
    add = {"Standard of care": 0.0, "Strategy A": p["c_trtA"],
           "Strategy B": p["c_trtB"], "Strategy AB": p["c_trtA"] + p["c_trtB"]}[strategy]
    cost = np.array([p["c_H"], p["c_S1"] + add, p["c_S2"] + add, 0.0])
    u_s1 = p["u_trtA"] if strategy in ("Strategy A", "Strategy AB") else p["u_S1"]
    return CohortSpec(P, cost, np.array([p["u_H"], u_s1, p["u_S2"], 0.0]))

engine = MarkovModel(states=STATES, strategies=STRATEGIES, model_fn=model,
                     n_cycles=75, start="H", half_cycle_correction="simpson")
```

## Parameter distributions

Each rate, cost, and utility gets a distribution reflecting its estimate. `ParameterSet.sample` returns a draw matrix indexed by the canonical `iteration` index; `SeedManager` makes the draw reproducible.

```{python}
from heormodel.params import Beta, Gamma, LogNormal, ParameterSet
from heormodel.run import SeedManager, run_psa

params = ParameterSet({
    "r_HD": Gamma(20, 1/10000), "r_HS1": Gamma(30, 1/200), "r_S1H": Gamma(60, 1/120),
    "r_S1S2": Gamma(84, 1/800), "hr_S1": LogNormal(np.log(3), 0.01),
    "hr_S2": LogNormal(np.log(10), 0.02), "hr_S1S2_trtB": LogNormal(np.log(0.6), 0.02),
    "c_H": Gamma(100, 20.0), "c_S1": Gamma(177.8, 22.5), "c_S2": Gamma(225, 66.7),
    "c_trtA": Gamma(73.5, 163.3), "c_trtB": Gamma(86.2, 150.8),
    "u_H": Beta(200, 3), "u_S1": Beta(130, 45), "u_S2": Beta(230, 230), "u_trtA": Beta(300, 15),
})
draws = params.sample(2_000, seed=SeedManager(20260705).generator())
draws.head(3).round(3)
```

## Run the PSA

`run_psa` evaluates the engine on every draw and returns `Outcomes` carrying exactly the draws' index, the linkage value-of-information analysis needs. It runs in parallel by default; this small run passes `sequential=True` to stay in-process and quiet.

```{python}
outcomes = run_psa(engine, draws, sequential=True)
outcomes.summary().round(2)
```

The `summary` means are the base of the cost-effectiveness ranking, and the spread across draws is the uncertainty the acceptability curve and value of information read.

## Convergence

`running_means` tracks the running mean of an outcome per strategy as iterations accumulate. Traces that flatten toward the right edge suggest the count is adequate for the means; value-of-information estimates typically need more.

```{python}
from heormodel.run import running_means

running_means(outcomes).plot(xlabel="iterations", ylabel="mean cost");
```

Next: [cost-effectiveness analysis](cea.qmd) ranks the strategies and builds acceptability curves from these outcomes.
